text: The Stoics distinguished between two uses of attention: prosoche, mindful watchfulness of one's own impressions, and phantasia, the raw stream of appearances presenting themselves to consciousness. Phantasia was morally neutral — appearances arrived unbidden, shaped by memory, habit, and desire. Prosoche was the distinctly human act: the pause between appearance and assent in which reason examined whether an impression corresponded to reality. Pierre Hadot, the twentieth-century scholar who recovered these practices for modern readers, called prosoche 'the fundamental Stoic spiritual exercise.' To live naturally, he argued, was to sustain this watchfulness habitually — not as a technique but as the basic posture of a mind functioning as it was designed to function.
question: According to the passage, what did Pierre Hadot call prosoche?

options.text: The raw stream of appearances presenting themselves to consciousness
The pause between sensation and rational judgment
The Stoic method for extinguishing unwanted desires
